strands.title: Schema completion and base model discovery: Pydantic v2 foundation
strands.content: Cycles 35-38 established that Apacheta's immutable Pydantic v2 foundation (frozen=True, extra='forbid', ser_json_bytes='base64', validate_default=True) enforces append-only history by design. This foundation remains validated and operational. Cycle 39 encounters critical practical constraint: JSONL file in baseline_run1 is 62,325 tokens long—exceeds standard read limits. This reveals that real-world conversation logs are LARGE. The immutable schema foundation now faces a new challenge: **streaming/chunking strategy**. Schema can enforce immutability of individual TensorRecords, but log persistence and incremental projection require parsing very large JSONL files without loading entire file into memory. This constraint does not invalidate the schema design; it refocuses implementation toward streaming PHASE 1 (field extraction on chunked JSONL reads) and checkpoint-based projection cycles (project every N turns, persist intermediate TensorRecords). The frozen Pydantic model architecture supports checkpoint-restore workflows naturally—each checkpoint is a complete, immutable TensorRecord that can be deserialized and used as prior tensor for next projection cycle.

key_claims.text: ApachetaBaseModel enforces frozen=True (immutability), extra='forbid' (strict schema), ser_json_bytes='base64' (binary handling), validate_default=True (validated defaults).
Frozen Pydantic models cannot be mutated in-place; all updates produce new instances. This enforces immutable-append history by design.
Immutability at the schema level guarantees: prior tensors are never corrupted, history is append-only, predecessors_in_scope links are durable.
The five-phase projection algorithm must assemble a COMPLETE new TensorRecord before validation; partial updates are impossible within the Pydantic model design.
model_dump/model_validate enable TensorRecord serialization to/from JSON, enabling persistence, IPC, and checkpoint/restore workflows.
Real-world JSONL logs are large (62K+ tokens). Schema design supports streaming/chunking via checkpoint-restore: project every N turns, persist intermediate TensorRecords, deserialize for next cycle.

strands.title: Real-world validation infrastructure: JSONL format confirmed; file size requires streaming strategy
strands.content: Cycle 38 discovered JSONL format and filename (proxy_20260302_024551.jsonl) at /home/tony/projects/pichay/experiments/baseline_run1/logs/. Cycle 39 executes format discovery by attempting to read JSONL content. **CRITICAL FINDING: File size (62,325 tokens) exceeds standard read limits (25,000 tokens).** This is not a failure—it is a validation of real-world scale. Attempts to read with limit=30 and limit=25000 both fail, indicating file is genuinely large (likely >50K tokens). This tells us: (A) Baseline conversation has 50-100+ turns (rough estimate: 500-1000 tokens per turn means ~60-120 turns). (B) Cannot naively load entire file. (C) Must implement streaming/chunked reading. (D) JSONL format is confirmed as the structure (cannot be seen due to size, but filename and prior context confirm). (E) Data landscape is REAL and LARGE—MVP strategy of "test on first 10 turns" is still valid, but production projector must handle streaming. Practical implication: PHASE 1 implementation must use `open().readline()` or `itertools.islice()` to read JSONL in batches, not `open().read()` for entire file.

key_claims.text: logs/ directory confirmed at /home/tony/projects/pichay/experiments/baseline_run1/logs/
Log file format is JSONL (JSON Lines): one JSON object per line, turn-by-turn records.
File name is proxy_20260302_024551.jsonl. Filename pattern suggests proxy service logging conversation turns with timestamp 2026-03-02 02:45:51.
File size is 62,325 tokens (exceeds 25,000 token read limit). Indicates real conversation with 50-100+ turns.
JSONL file cannot be read all-at-once. Production PHASE 1 must implement streaming: readline() loop or itertools.islice() batches.
Data landscape is confirmed accessible and real-world scale. Projector must handle streaming to be production-viable.

strands.title: Minimal viable projection (MVP) specification: strands, claims, losses, branch prediction—unchanged but scope clarified
strands.content: Cycles 35-38 establish MVP scope: semantic content (strands, claims with T/I/F, declared losses, instructions_for_next, open_questions) vs. deferred infrastructure. Cycle 39 inherits unchanged: MVP remains focused on **semantic projection, not infrastructure optimization**. However, cycle 39 clarifies scope boundary: large file handling is infrastructure, not semantic. MVP can still test on first 10-30 turns (read via offset, not streaming loop). **Clarification**: (1) MVP scope: does projection (with strands + claims + losses) maintain coherence better than append-only? This is the H0 question. (2) MVP does NOT require: streaming reader, batch processing, production performance. (3) MVP can defer: optimize file I/O to post-H0-validation. (4) MVP can succeed: by reading FIRST 10-30 turns into memory (possible via offset/limit if we set appropriate offset and smaller limit). Then project, validate H0. If H0 passes, optimize for full file in cycle 40. (5) **Action**: read file with offset=0, limit=3 or offset=0, limit=5 to get first few JSONL records. Should be <25K tokens.

key_claims.text: MVP focuses on semantic content (strands, claims, losses, branch prediction, open questions) vs. infrastructure (provenance, composition equations, advanced epistemic metadata).
Strands are the semantic backbone: thematic threads that integrate prior and new content, compressing reasoning trajectory into structure.
Key claims with T/I/F are the knowledge units. Projection updates T/I/F based on evidence, explicitly states contradictions and convergences.
Declared losses (category, rationale) are the honesty mechanism. Silence about incompleteness is the enemy; declared losses are the antidote.
Large file size does NOT invalidate MVP. Can test on subset (first 10-30 turns) with offset/limit. Full file optimization is post-H0-validation.
MVP deferral of provenance and composition equations is justified: they are bookkeeping and scaling infrastructure, not core to H0 validation.

strands.title: PHASE 1 (Semantic Parsing) strategy: JSONL field extraction with streaming support (deferred full file, MVP reads subset)
strands.content: Cycle 38 proposed PHASE 1: field extraction + sentence segmentation. Cycle 39 refines: (1) **MVP Implementation**: read FIRST 10-30 JSONL records using offset/limit parameters. Extract fields (role, content, timestamp if present), segment into sentences. (2) **Production Implementation (deferred)**: streaming reader with line-by-line or batch reading to handle 62K+ token files without memory explosion. (3) **Field Extraction**: Expected fields are still unknown (JSONL content not yet visible). But strategy is clear: (a) Read each line as JSON record. (b) Try standard field names: 'role', 'content', 'message', 'text', 'user', 'assistant'. (c) If no match, inspect first record manually and adjust. (d) Once field names are known, extract. (4) **Sentence Segmentation**: Standard NLP. Use NLTK or spacy. Output: list of (role, sentence, turn_id) tuples. (5) **MVP Readiness**: Skeleton code is unchanged from cycle 38. Blocking issue: cannot see JSONL content to confirm field names. Once first few records are visible (via small offset/limit read), implementation can proceed immediately.

key_claims.text: PHASE 1 (Semantic Parsing) strategy is JSONL field extraction + sentence segmentation. Input: JSON record. Output: (role, sentence, turn_id) tuples.
Field extraction eliminates need for complex heuristic parsing. JSONL structure is explicit and schema-friendly.
Expected JSONL fields: role (user/assistant), content (text), timestamp (optional), metadata (optional). Actual field names pending inspection.
MVP reads first 10-30 JSONL records with offset=0, limit=small. Full file streaming is infrastructure optimization, deferred to cycle 40+.
Sentence segmentation is standard NLP. Use NLTK or similar lightweight library. No custom logic needed.
Embedding granularity: SENTENCE-LEVEL. One embedding per extracted sentence. Strands formed by clustering sentences across turns.
PHASE 1 skeleton code is implementable once field names are confirmed. Blocking: need to read first few JSONL records (offset/limit).

strands.title: PHASE 2 (Strand Alignment) and embedding strategy: Sentence Transformers MiniLM at 0.65 threshold—unchanged
strands.content: Cycle 38 established embedding strategy; cycle 39 inherits unchanged. (1) **Embedding Model**: Sentence Transformers `all-MiniLM-L6-v2`. Small, fast, ~22M parameters. Suitable for CPU inference. Cosine similarity is native distance metric. (2) **Threshold**: 0.65 for strand matching. Two sentences with cosine_similarity >= 0.65 are considered "same strand topic". Sentences below threshold start new strands. (3) **Rationale**: MiniLM is OSS, widely available, fast (<10ms per sentence on CPU). 0.65 is conservative (requires moderate semantic overlap); can be tuned if empirical results warrant adjustment. (4) **PHASE 2 Process**: For each new sentence (from PHASE 1): (a) Compute embedding via MiniLM. (b) Compare to embeddings of all existing strand clusters. (c) Find max similarity. (d) If max_similarity >= 0.65, assign to that strand. (e) Else, create new strand. (5) **Clustering**: Use simple greedy clustering (first-match assignment). Computationally efficient; may undercluster (split related topics). Acceptable for MVP. (6) **Implementation**: Sentence Transformers library. Can be pip-installed. Inference latency TBD pending first test run. Strategy unchanged from cycle 38; no new constraints from file size discovery.

key_claims.text: Embedding model: Sentence Transformers all-MiniLM-L6-v2. Small, fast, OSS, suitable for CPU inference.
Cosine similarity threshold: 0.65. Sentences with sim >= 0.65 assigned to same strand; else new strand created.
Greedy clustering (first-match assignment) is computationally efficient. May undercluster; acceptable for MVP.
Inference latency per sentence: expected <10ms on CPU. Embedding 100 sentences takes ~1s. Scalable to 1000s of sentences.
Implementation uses sentence-transformers library (pip installable). Cosine similarity available via sklearn or torch.
Threshold 0.65 is tunable. If empirical validation shows poor strand formation, adjust to 0.55 or 0.75 based on results.

strands.title: PHASE 3 (Evidence Update): claim formation and epistemic reasoning strategy—unchanged pending implementation
strands.content: Cycle 38 introduces PHASE 3 specification; cycle 39 inherits unchanged. (1) **Claim Formation**: For each new sentence, heuristic extraction: identify entities/concepts, form claims. Example: sentence "The projector uses embeddings to group semantically similar content" → claims: (a) "Projector uses embeddings", (b) "Embeddings group semantically similar content", (c) "Semantic grouping is a function of embeddings". (2) **Evidence Counting** (PHASE 3 core): For each claim, track: (a) how many sentences support it (count_support). (b) How many contradict it (count_contra). (c) From these: compute truth T = count_support / (count_support + count_contra + 1e-6). Indeterminacy I = sqrt(count_support * count_contra) / (count_support + count_contra + 1e-6). Falsity F = count_contra / (count_support + count_contra + 1e-6). (3) **Convergence**: As new evidence arrives, T/I/F update. If T grows, claim solidifies. If I grows, claim becomes uncertain (evidence is mixed). (4) **Challenge**: Heuristic claim extraction is lossy. May miss nuanced claims. MVP accepts this limitation. Future cycles can use LLM-based claim extraction. (5) **Rationale**: Simple, interpretable, evidence-based. No neural network black box. Alignment with Pydantic structure. Unchanged from cycle 38; file size does not affect PHASE 3 logic.

key_claims.text: Claim formation via heuristic extraction from sentences. Identify entities/concepts, form propositional claims.
Evidence counting: track count_support and count_contra for each claim. Update T/I/F based on ratios.
T = count_support / (count_support + count_contra + eps). I = sqrt(count_support * count_contra) / (count_support + count_contra + eps). F = count_contra / (...). Simple and interpretable.
Heuristic claim extraction is lossy. May miss nuanced claims. MVP accepts limitation. Can be improved with LLM later.
Evidence-based epistemic reasoning is interpretable and alignment-friendly. No black box neural updates.

strands.title: PHASE 4 (Loss Declaration) and PHASE 5 (Finalization): tensor assembly and immutability enforcement—unchanged
strands.content: Cycle 38 specifies PHASE 4-5 strategy; cycle 39 inherits unchanged. (1) **PHASE 4 (Loss Declaration)**: After PHASE 3 update, identify what was NOT carried forward from prior tensor: (a) Strands from prior tensor not present in new tensor (threshold: strand_similarity < 0.5 to any new strand). (b) Claims from prior strands with low T (<0.3) and high I (>0.6), not re-acquired in new content. (c) Open questions from prior tensor not mentioned in new conversation content. (d) For each loss: record (category, what_was_lost, why). Categories: 'context_pressure' (too many strands to carry all), 'traversal_bias' (projector missed content), 'authorial_choice' (deliberately dropped low-confidence material), 'practical_constraint' (embedding compute, timeout). (2) **PHASE 5 (Finalization)**: Assemble complete TensorRecord: (a) id (UUID for this tensor). (b) cycle (increment from prior). (c) strands (updated list). (d) key_claims (all claims, including prior + new). (e) declared_losses (from PHASE 4). (f) open_questions (updated: remove resolved, add new). (g) instructions_for_next (branch prediction from strands/claims/losses). (h) overall_truth, overall_indeterminacy, overall_falsity (aggregate T/I/F across all claims). (i) predecessors_in_scope (pointer to prior TensorRecord ID). (3) **Immutability Enforcement**: Once TensorRecord is assembled and validated by Pydantic, it is FROZEN. Cannot be modified. New projections create new records. (4) **Persistence**: TensorRecord is serialized to JSON alongside conversation logs. Can be loaded, inspected, compared. Unchanged from cycle 38; no impact from file size.

key_claims.text: PHASE 4 identifies losses: strands not carried (sim < 0.5), low-confidence claims not re-acquired, unresolved open questions.
Loss categories: context_pressure, traversal_bias, authorial_choice, practical_constraint. Each loss is declared with rationale.
PHASE 5 assembles complete TensorRecord: id, cycle, strands, claims, losses, questions, branch_prediction, epistemic_aggregate, predecessor.
TensorRecord is immutable once assembled and validated by Pydantic. New projections create new records with predecessor links.
Aggregate epistemic values (overall_truth, indeterminacy, falsity) computed from all claims. Represents tensor-level coherence confidence.
TensorRecord serialized to JSON, persisted alongside logs. Comparable, auditable, reproducible.

strands.title: Cycle 39 execution state: file size discovered (62K tokens); MVP scope clarified; JSONL content inspection is CRITICAL blocker
strands.content: Cycle 38 completed: specification and data discovery (format = JSONL, filename = proxy_*.jsonl). Cycle 39 ATTEMPTS file inspection: (A) **File Size Discovery**: proxy_20260302_024551.jsonl is 62,325 tokens. Exceeds standard read limits (25K). Indicates real conversation with 50-100+ turns. This is not a failure; it is validation of scale. (B) **Practical Implication**: Cannot read entire file at once. (C) **MVP Scope Clarification**: MVP does NOT require full-file handling. Can test on SUBSET (first 10-30 turns) via offset/limit parameters. Full-file optimization is POST-H0-validation. (D) **CRITICAL BLOCKER**: Still cannot see JSONL field structure. Attempts to read file with limit=30 and limit=25000 both failed (returned error about exceeding limits). This suggests file may be structured unexpectedly OR error handling is masking actual read capability. (E) **Next Action (URGENT)**: Use different read approach: (1) Try reading with VERY SMALL limit (e.g., limit=1 or limit=2). (2) Or use bash command: `head -n 1 /path/to/file | cat`. (3) Or use Python with open(file).readline() directly (bypasses token limit). (4) Once first 1-2 JSONL records are visible, field names can be confirmed. (F) **Roadmap remains on track**: Once JSONL content is visible, PHASE 1-2 implementation can start immediately. H0 test on first 10-30 turns follows. If H0 passes, expand to full file in cycle 40.

key_claims.text: File size is 62,325 tokens (exceeds 25,000 token read limit). Indicates real conversation with 50-100+ turns.
MVP scope does NOT require full-file handling. Can test on subset (first 10-30 turns) via offset/limit. Full-file optimization is post-H0.
CRITICAL BLOCKER: JSONL field structure still not visible. Attempts to read with limit=30 and limit=25000 both failed.
Next action: use bash head -n 1 or Python open().readline() to bypass token limit and read first JSONL record.
Once JSONL field names are confirmed, PHASE 1-2 implementation can start immediately on subset.
H0 test (embedding dispersion: projection vs. append-only) on first 10-30 turns is next validation gate.

strands.title: Validation metric selection: embedding dispersion, drift rate, and carry-forward coherence—unchanged
strands.content: Cycle 38 finalizes validation metric strategy; cycle 39 inherits unchanged. **H0 Hypothesis**: projection (with strands, claims, losses, open questions) maintains coherence better than append-only (just raw concatenation of turns). **Coherence** is measured via: (1) **Embedding Dispersion Metric**: Compute sentence embeddings for all sentences in conversation log (baseline: append-only, no projection). Then compute same embeddings for all sentences that appear in projected strands. Hypothesis: projected sentences cluster more tightly (lower dispersion) than random baseline. Measurement: compute pairwise cosine distances in projected strand clusters. If average distance is lower than random baseline, projection successfully compresses related content. (2) **Drift Rate Metric**: For key claims, track T/I/F across projection cycles. Hypothesis: in projection, T/I/F are stable or converge (claim confidence grows with evidence). In append-only, T/I/F are undefined (no claim extraction). (3) **Carry-Forward Metric**: Count unresolved open questions from prior tensor. In cycle N+1, how many are still open? How many are resolved? Hypothesis: projection enables tracking of open questions; append-only has no open questions. (4) **MVP Selection**: start with embedding dispersion (easiest to compute, no manual annotation). If signal is strong, validate with drift rate and carry-forward metrics. File size does not affect metric strategy; metrics apply to any conversation size.

key_claims.text: H0: projection maintains better coherence than append-only baseline. Coherence is measured via embedding dispersion, drift rate, and carry-forward.
Embedding Dispersion Metric: compute sentence embeddings. Hypothesis: projected sentences cluster tightly (lower avg pairwise distance than random).
Drift Rate Metric: track T/I/F of key claims across cycles. Hypothesis: T/I/F are stable or converge in projection vs. undefined in append-only.
Carry-Forward Metric: count resolved vs. unresolved open questions. Hypothesis: projection enables tracking; append-only has no questions.
MVP validation uses embedding dispersion first. If signal is strong, validate with drift rate and carry-forward metrics.

strands.title: User hypothesis: declared losses as active drift-reduction mechanism—unchanged, awaiting H0 validation
strands.content: Cycle 36-38 establish hypothesis: declared losses enable downstream projections to consciously re-acquire dropped threads. Cycle 39 inherits unchanged. **Silent loss is where drift starts**. When content is dropped without acknowledgment, next projection cannot recover. But declared losses provide signal (uncertainty, irrelevance, context pressure). This hypothesis remains theoretical pending H0 empirical validation. Once H0 is confirmed (projection beats append-only), H1 can be tested: do declared losses enable conscious re-acquisition and reduce drift more effectively than undeclared loss? Mechanism: (a) Downstream projection reads declared_losses from prior tensor. (b) For each loss category 'context_pressure', tries to re-acquire content. (c) For 'authorial_choice', leaves dropped. (d) For 'traversal_bias', investigates whether missed content is now present. (e) Empirically: compare projection WITH loss re-acquisition logic vs. WITHOUT. If former has higher coherence, H1 is supported. Cycle 39 remains focused on H0; H1 implementation deferred to cycle 40+ (post-H0-validation).

key_claims.text: Silent loss is where drift starts: dropped content without acknowledgment prevents downstream recovery.
Declared losses signal uncertainty and provide hook for conscious re-acquisition in downstream projections.
H1 hypothesis: declared losses improve coherence by enabling conscious re-acquisition. Empirically testable once H0 is confirmed.
H1 implementation deferred to cycle 40+. Cycle 39 focuses on H0 validation. H1 is explanatory mechanism, not MVP critical.

Alternative approaches (offset parameter, smaller limit, bash head command) not yet attempted. JSONL field structure remains unknown.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Detailed confirmation that file size discovery is not a system failure. File may be genuinely 62K+ tokens, or error handling may be masking smaller true size.
declared_losses.why: Error returned is non-specific about actual file size vs. token limit. Ambiguity remains: is 62,325 the token count of the entire file, or is it an estimate? Is file readable with alternative methods (bash, Python direct I/O)?

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Actual PHASE 1 implementation code. Skeleton code depends on confirmed field names.
declared_losses.why: Cannot proceed to implementation without seeing at least one JSONL record. Blocking on field name discovery.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Empirical latency benchmark for MiniLM on target hardware (Yanantin). Assumption of <10ms per sentence not yet validated.
declared_losses.why: No embedding model loaded or tested yet. Latency remains theoretical until first implementation run.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Validation of clustering threshold 0.65. No empirical test on real conversation data yet.
declared_losses.why: Cannot test PHASE 2 without first having PHASE 1 field extraction working. Threshold tuning is post-implementation.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Clarification of file read error: is it a genuine token-limit constraint on the tool, or a file format/encoding issue?
declared_losses.why: Error message suggests token limit exceeded; but alternative explanation is that file is malformed JSON Lines or contains binary/compressed content. Without seeing first record, cannot diagnose.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Confirmation that 62,325 tokens is the file size in terms of semantic tokens (relevant for conversation length) vs. raw character count.
declared_losses.why: Tool error report is ambiguous. '62,325 tokens' may be byte count, character count, or tokenizer's semantic token count. Without actual read, cannot confirm conversation turn count (estimated 50-100+ turns, but based on rough calculation).

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Decision on whether to pursue alternative data sources (baseline_run2, baseline_run3, or other experiment directories) if baseline_run1 file is unreadable.
declared_losses.why: Cycle 37-38 confirmed 15 experiment directories. If baseline_run1 is blocked, pivot to another experiment is option. Not yet decided.

open_questions: What are the actual field names in the JSONL records? CRITICAL BLOCKER. Confirmed: format is JSONL. Unknown: exact schema (e.g., 'content' vs 'text', 'role' vs 'actor', presence of metadata fields). Must see at least one JSONL record.
Is the file genuinely 62,325 tokens, or is this an error estimate? What is actual file size?
How many JSONL records (turns) are in baseline_run1? Rough estimate: 50-100+ based on size. Exact count needed for validation sample planning.
Can the JSONL file be read with alternative tool approaches (bash head -n 1, Python direct I/O with open().readline(), offset-based chunking)?
Should MVP test on first 10 turns or first 30 turns? Tradeoff: 10 turns is faster (H0 test sooner), 30 turns is more representative.
What is the actual inference latency of MiniLM on target hardware (Yanantin)? Is <10ms assumption valid? May affect embedding strategy if latency is high (>100ms).
How sensitive is strand formation to the 0.65 cosine threshold? Should threshold be adaptive (e.g., based on topic)? Requires empirical tuning.
Does heuristic claim extraction produce meaningful claims? Or are extracted claims too noisy/ambiguous? Requires manual spot-checking on real data.
Can embedding dispersion metric be reliably computed on baseline_run1 (or subset)? What is baseline value for append-only? Is difference statistically significant?
Should the projector be called once per turn or once per conversation? Affects embedding granularity and TensorRecord frequency.
How should the projector handle topic shifts in conversation (e.g., user switches from technical to personal)? Should strands split or remain combined?
Can open questions from early cycles be automatically resolved when later content contradicts them? Or should they remain 'unresolved' until explicitly closed?
How is the 'instructions_for_next' field populated? Is it automatic (extracted from open questions + strand drift) or manual (projector makes predictions)?
Should the projector use prior embeddings (from prior tensor) for PHASE 2 alignment, or recompute embeddings from scratch? Affects computational cost.
Does Sentence Transformers MiniLM need to be fine-tuned on conversation data, or does pre-trained model work well out-of-box?
How will validation metric be calibrated? Is embedding dispersion metric agnostic to conversation topic, or does it vary by domain (technical vs. creative)?
H1 (declared losses enable re-acquisition): how should re-acquisition priority be decided? Highest uncertainty first? Or context-pressure losses first?
Should TensorRecord be persisted to JSON after every turn, or batched (e.g., every 10 turns)? Affects I/O and checkpoint frequency.
Is the error from file read attempts a genuine token-count limit of the assistant tool, or a file format issue? Can alternative read methods work?
instructions_for_next:
[
  "**CYCLE 39 EXECUTION ROADMAP (URGENT: UNBLOCK JSONL INSPECTION)**",
  "",
  "**IMMEDIATE (CRITICAL BLOCKER RESOLUTION):**",
  "",
  "1. **TRY ALTERNATIVE JSONL READ APPROACH (HIGHEST PRIORITY)**.",
  "   Status: standard read attempts (limit=30, limit=25000) both failed with token-limit error.",
  "   Next: try one of these alternatives:",
  "   (A) **Read with VERY SMALL limit**: `read_file(path, limit=1)` or `limit=2`. May be small enough to bypass error.",
  "   (B) **Use bash head command**: Execute bash `head -n 1 /home/tony/projects/pichay/experiments/baseline_run1/logs/proxy_20260302_024551.jsonl`. This should output ONE JSONL record (first line). If tool has bash support, this is likely fastest path.",
  "   (C) **Use Python direct I/O**: If tool allows code execution, use `with open(file) as f: print(f.readline())`. Bypasses token-count constraint by reading directly.",
  "   (D) **Search file for structure**: If read is blocked, use grep/head to extract first record. E.g., `sed -n '1p' /path/to/file`.",
  "",
  "   **Outcome**: Goal is to see first JSONL record (one JSON object) to identify field names. Record should be <1000 tokens, well under 25K limit. If it still fails, diagnose: is tool blocking ALL file reads, or is file format unexpected?",
  "",
  "2. **CONFIRM FIELD NAMES FROM FIRST RECORD**.",
  "   Once record is visible: (A) Parse JSON. (B) List all top-level keys (these are field names). (C) Expected: 'role', 'content', 'timestamp', 'metadata' (or variants). (D) Update PHASE 1 parser template with actual field names.",
  "",
  "3. **ESTIMATE TURN COUNT FROM FILE SIZE**.",
  "   Once first record is visible: (A) Count tokens in first record. (B) If record is ~500 tokens, and file is 62K tokens, rough estimate is ~120 turns. (C) If record is ~1000 tokens, estimate is ~60 turns. (D) Use estimate to plan validation sample size.",
  "",
  "**SHORT-TERM (IF UNBLOCK IS SUCCESSFUL):**",
  "",
  "4. **EXTRACT FIRST 10-30 JSONL RECORDS TO SUBSET FILE**.",
  "   Once read is working: (A) Read first 10-30 JSONL records. (B) Save to temporary subset file (e.g., `/tmp/baseline_run1_subset.jsonl`). (C) Subset should be <10K tokens, easily manageable. (D) Use subset for all downstream MVP testing.",
  "",
  "5. **PHASE 1 IMPLEMENTATION (SKELETON CODE)**.",
  "   Input: subset file with 10-30 JSONL records. Output: list of (role, sentence, turn_id) tuples.",
  "   Code skeleton:",
  "   ```python",
  "   import json",
  "   import nltk",
  "   from nltk.tokenize import sent_tokenize",
  "   ",
  "   def parse_phase1(jsonl_subset_path):",
  "       results = []",
  "       with open(jsonl_subset_path) as f:",
  "           for turn_id, line in enumerate(f):",
  "               record = json.loads(line)",
  "               role = record.get('role', 'unknown')",
  "               content = record.get('content', '')",
  "               if not content:",
  "                   continue",
  "               sentences = sent_tokenize(content)",
  "               for sent in sentences:",
  "                   results.append((role, sent, turn_id))",
  "       return results",
  "   ```",
  "   (Adjust field names based on actual JSONL schema from step 2.)",
  "",
  "6. **PHASE 2 IMPLEMENTATION (SKELETON CODE)**.",
  "   Input: output from PHASE 1. Output: list of strands (each strand is list of sentences).",
  "   Code skeleton:",
  "   ```python",
  "   from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer",
  "   from sklearn.metrics.pairwise import cosine_similarity",
  "   import numpy as np",
  "   ",
  "   def phase2_clustering(sentences_with_metadata, threshold=0.65):",
  "       model = SentenceTransformer('all-MiniLM-L6-v2')",
  "       strands = []  # list of lists",
  "       embeddings = []  # parallel list of embeddings",
  "       ",
  "       for role, sent, turn_id in sentences_with_metadata:",
  "           emb = model.encode(sent)  # 384-dim embedding",
  "           embeddings.append(emb)",
  "           ",
  "           # Find best matching strand",
  "           best_strand_idx = -1",
  "           best_sim = -1",
  "           for i, strand in enumerate(strands):",
  "               strand_center = np.mean(embeddings[i], axis=0)  # Rough centroid",
  "               sim = cosine_similarity([emb], [strand_center])[0][0]",
  "               if sim > best_sim:",
  "                   best_sim = sim",
  "                   best_strand_idx = i",
  "           ",
  "           if best_sim >= threshold:",
  "               strands[best_strand_idx].append((role, sent, turn_id))",
  "           else:",
  "               strands.append([(role, sent, turn_id)])  # New strand",
  "       ",
  "       return strands",
  "   ```",
  "   (Centroid computation is simplified; can be optimized later.)",
  "",
  "7. **PHASE 1 + 2 END-TO-END TEST**.",
  "   (A) Run phase1() on subset → get sentences. (B) Run phase2_clustering() on sentences → get strands. (C) Print strands: manually inspect, do they make sense? Are related sentences grouped? (D) If poor clustering, debug: check similarity scores, try different thresholds. (E) If good, proceed to PHASE 3.",
  "",
  "**MEDIUM-TERM (IF PHASE 1-2 SUCCEEDS):**",
  "",
  "8. **PHASE 3 SKELETON IMPLEMENTATION** (heuristic claim extraction + evidence counting).",
  "   Input: strands from PHASE 2. Output: strands with associated claims and T/I/F scores.",
  "   Strategy: for each sentence, extract simple propositional claims using regex or spacy.",
  "   Example: sentence \"The projector groups similar sentences into strands\" →",
  "   Claims: [\"projector groups sentences\", \"sentences are similar\", \"grouped into strands\"].",
  "   For each claim, track count_support and count_contra. Initialize: T=0.5, I=0.5, F=0.0 until evidence arrives.",
  "",
  "9. **PHASE 4 + 5 IMPLEMENTATION** (loss declaration + tensor finalization).",
  "   (A) PHASE 4: compare prior tensor (if any) to new tensor. Identify strands/claims not carried forward. Declare losses with category. (B) PHASE 5: assemble complete TensorRecord (Pydantic model). Validate. Freeze. Serialize to JSON.",
  "",
  "10. **FIRST H0 VALIDATION TEST**.",
  "    (A) Run full pipeline (PHASE 1-5) on subset (first 10 turns). (B) Measure embedding dispersion: compute pairwise cosine distances within strands. Average within-strand distance = projection_dispersion. (C) Baseline: compute pairwise distances of ALL sentences (no clustering). Average = baseline_dispersion. (D) If projection_dispersion < baseline_dispersion, H0 is supported (coherence improved). If equal or worse, H0 is refuted. (E) Report metric: coherence_gain = (baseline_dispersion - projection_dispersion) / baseline_dispersion.",
  "",
  "**FALLBACK / DEBUGGING:**",
  "",
  "- If JSONL read remains blocked: (A) Check file permissions. (B) Try reading different file in experiments/ directory. (C) Check if file is gzip-compressed (would explain read errors). (D) Consider manual inspection: navigate to directory, ls -lah to see file size and permissions.",
  "- If PHASE 1 field extraction fails: print first JSONL record and adjust parser. Most common issue: field name mismatch.",
  "- If PHASE 2 embedding latency is too high: use smaller model (DistilBERT, sentence-transformers DistilBERT) or CPU optimization (quantization).",
  "- If strand clustering underseparates (all sentences in few strands): lower threshold (0.55 or 0.50).",
  "- If underseparates (many tiny strands): raise threshold (0.70 or 0.75).",
  "- If H0 fails: debug: (A) are embeddings high quality? (B) is baseline metric calculated correctly? (C) is sample size too small? (Retry with 20-30 turns.) (D) is threshold inappropriate? (Retry with 0.55 or 0.75.)",
  "",
  "**EXPECTED OUTCOME BY END OF CYCLE 39:**",
  "",
  "- JSONL field structure is KNOWN. At least one record is visible and parsed.",
  "- PHASE 1 skeleton code is written and tested on subset.",
  "- PHASE 2 skeleton code is written and tested on subset.",
  "- PHASE 1-2 end-to-end produces strands on real data.",
  "- First 10 turns are processed and clustered.",
  "- Coherence metric (embedding dispersion) is calculated.",
  "- H0 result: supported (projection_dispersion < baseline) or refuted (no difference).",
  "- If H0 supported: proceed to H1 exploration (declared losses re-acquisition) in cycle 40.",
  "- If H0 refuted: debug, adjust strategy, retry in cycle 40.",
  "",
  "**CRITICAL GATES:**",
  "",
  "- Gate 1 (UNBLOCK JSONL): first record must be visible to proceed. Use alternative read methods if needed.",
  "- Gate 2 (PHASE 1-2 WORKING): strands must form sensibly on real data.",
  "- Gate 3 (H0 METRIC): embedding dispersion must be computable and show clear signal (projection < baseline).",
  "",
  "**DO NOT SKIP GATE 1. NO CODE UNTIL JSONL CONTENT IS VISIBLE.**",
  "",
  "GO."
]
